<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- agent-browser: Browser automation using Vercel's agent-browser CLI. Use when you need to interact with web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, or scrape data. Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-browser/SKILL.md)
- agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop. (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-native-architecture/SKILL.md)

- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.text:
**Planning to separate Vitest imports from test-utils**

The test-utils package currently imports vitest/jest-dom matchers which might cause conflicts or redundancy since apps/org imports "@testing-library/jest-dom" differently. Creating a dedicated Vitest entrypoint for test-utils will isolate Vitest-specific setup and avoid unintended side effects in other contexts like Playwright or production code.

**Investigating Playwright test runner and test-utils interaction**

The error might stem from Playwright running e2e tasks in multiple packages, potentially loading test-utils indirectly via app code or global setup. Next, I'll check Playwright’s tsconfig and global setup to see if test-utils or its Vitest-specific parts are loaded during Playwright runs.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". - Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.

  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

**Analyzing test import conflicts**

I'm identifying that importing `@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest` via shared test-utils causes conflicts with Playwright's `expect` in e2e tests, suggesting conditionally importing it only during Vitest runs or separating the vitest-specific imports to avoid overlap. Providing debug commands and recommending isolating these imports will help clarify and resolve the expect redefinition issue.

**Clarifying e2e import conflicts**

The error typically means Vitest’s expect is loaded alongside Playwright’s due to importing `@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest` (often via shared test-utils) in e2e tests. I’ll recommend isolating which e2e suite fails, checking for such imports, and moving vitest-specific matchers into a separate module imported only by Vitest setup files. I can provide a fix for this if the user wants and ask for stack trace details to confirm.

payload.message:
This error usually means Vitest’s expect is being installed on top of an existing expect (Playwright/Jest), which throws when the `$$jest-matchers-object` symbol is redefined. The most common cause is importing `@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest` (directly or via `@workspace/test-utils`) inside a Playwright run.

Quick isolate:
- Run `pnpm --filter public-pages e2e` and `pnpm --filter @workspace/playwright e2e` to see which task trips it.
- If it only fails in the Playwright run, check whether any e2e helpers import `@workspace/test-utils` (that package currently imports `@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest` as a side effect in `packages/test-utils/src/index.ts`).

Fix options (pick one):
1) **Keep Playwright clean:** don’t import `@workspace/test-utils` or `@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest` anywhere in e2e tests.  
2) **Split Vitest-only setup:** move the vitest matcher import out of `@workspace/test-utils` and into a Vitest-only entrypoint, then import that in your Vitest `setupFiles`. This avoids Vitest installing expect during Playwright runs.

If you want, I can implement option 2. To do that I’d:
- Create `packages/test-utils/src/vitest.ts` with `import "@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest";`
- Remove that import from `packages/test-utils/src/index.ts`
- Add the new entry to each Vitest setup file (e.g., `apps/org/vitest.config.ts`, `apps/builder/vitest.config.ts`, `apps/public-pages/vitest.config.ts`)

Tell me which option you prefer, and if you have the full stack trace I can confirm the exact import path.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- agent-browser: Browser automation using Vercel's agent-browser CLI. Use when you need to interact with web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, or scrape data. Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-browser/SKILL.md)
- agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop. (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-native-architecture/SKILL.md)

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". ### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.

  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.

- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)

- LS: use ls via shell_command

- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". - Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.

- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.

- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.

- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". ### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.text:
**Analyzing jest-dom import conflicts**

I'm uncovering duplicate jest-dom extensions causing Symbol redefinition errors in Vitest, likely from importing plain jest-dom in test-utils alongside jest-dom/vitest in setup files. Next, I'll align all jest-dom imports to the Vitest variant and centralize setup to avoid duplicates.

summary.text:
**Analyzing jest-dom import conflicts**

I'm uncovering duplicate jest-dom extensions causing Symbol redefinition errors in Vitest, likely from importing plain jest-dom in test-utils alongside jest-dom/vitest in setup files. Next, I'll align all jest-dom imports to the Vitest variant and centralize setup to avoid duplicates.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:

- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test-storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.

- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
